mappings.rationaleAI: Directly covers the hominid fossil record, Australopithecus, Homo habilis, Homo erectus, Homo sapiens, and primate evolutionary relationships (A through C).
Covers natural selection, genetic drift, and speciation -- the core evolutionary mechanisms underlying the evolutionary process (A) and hominid divergence.
Covers Darwin's theory of natural and sexual selection, the foundational framework for understanding human evolutionary relationships (A, B).
Covers the discovery and interpretation of fossil remains, directly relevant to the hominid fossil record and paleontological evidence (C.1).
Covers Australopithecus through the emergence of Homo sapiens, Neanderthals, and Cro-Magnons in their environmental context (C.2).
Primates are mammals; covers mammalian brain evolution, behavior, and fossil record providing context for primate natural history (B.1.b, B.1.c).
Dental morphology is a key morphological characteristic used to classify hominid fossils and infer feeding adaptations (B.2.a, C.2).
Covers the genetic basis of evolutionary change, relevant to understanding molecular evidence for primate and hominid phylogenetics (A, B.1).
Ancient DNA and genomic sequencing have transformed understanding of evolutionary relationships among hominid lineages (B, C.2).
Provides the broad evolutionary context for the emergence of primates and hominids, including molecular clock dating (A, B.1.c).
Covers instinct, learning, and kin selection relevant to inferred behavioral characteristics of hominids and primate behavior (B.2.b).
Sexual dimorphism and mate choice are important for understanding contrasting morphological adaptations of Hominidae vs. Pongidae (B.2.c).
Covers megafauna extinction and Pleistocene events, relevant to the disappearance of Neanderthals and other hominid species (C.2).
Covers methods for excavating and interpreting early human sites, directly relevant to discovery and recognition of the hominid fossil record (C.1).
Addresses species concepts and essentialism relevant to hominid taxonomy and the classification of primates and Hominidae (B.1.d, C.2).
Covers geographic distribution of species relevant to migration patterns of hominids out of Africa and primate distribution (C.2.d.iv).
Covers the role of fire in human evolution, a key inferred behavioral characteristic distinguishing early Homo species (B.2.b).
Explains how Hox genes and developmental processes produce the morphological differences distinguishing Hominidae from Pongidae (B.2.a, B.2.c).
